menu.pat: Probably A Table npm Case Study

sudoku.title: Sudoku Board Generator (TDD, typing, architecture)
sudoku.intro1: This case study presents my real TDD workflow while building a Sudoku board generator.
sudoku.intro2: The entire process was recorded as an 8‑hour timelapse. Below I highlight the most important fragments — the ones that best show how I think, design, and iterate.
sudoku.plan1: I start with an analog sketch — outlining the structure of the problem, dependencies, and first ideas for the API. This helps me set priorities and break the work into small, testable steps.
sudoku.plan2: Then I move everything into Jira, where I create tasks and estimate them. This keeps the workflow organized and gives me a clear picture of what I’m doing and why.
sudoku.rowCheck1: I begin with the simplest element — a function that validates a Sudoku row. Following TDD principles:
sudoku.list_rowCheck1: I create a function rowCheck(row: number[]) that initially just returns false
sudoku.rowCheck2: I also considered testing non‑numeric values, but strong typing prevents such cases at compile time.
sudoku.rowCheck3: This is a great example of how types reduce the number of tests and speed up development.
sudoku.twoVersions1: During development I realize I need two variants:
sudoku.list_twoVersions1: rowCheck for the generator — it must require complete data (1–9).
sudoku.list_twoVersions2: rowCheck for the player — it must accept empty fields.
sudoku.twoVersions2: I add a separate Jira task to keep track of this and update the JSDocs to clearly describe the purpose of both functions.
sudoku.implementation1: I implement the logic: filtering elements and checking whether each appears exactly once.
sudoku.implementation2: A simple, clean function that is easy to test.
sudoku.architecture1: At this point I notice that I will need similar logic for:
sudoku.architecture2: Instead of duplicating code, I refactor the architecture:
sudoku.list4_architecture: separate data extractors for rows, columns, and sub‑squares.
sudoku.architecture3: This reduces complexity and makes testing easier.
sudoku.h_colExtractor: Column extractor tests and domain types
sudoku.colExtractor1: I start with a test: I expect the column extractor to return the correct array.
sudoku.colExtractor2: At this point I introduce domain‑specific types, such as Digit1to9, to:
sudoku.list1_colExtractor: limit the range of allowed values,
sudoku.subsquares1: I write an initial draft of the sub‑square extractor — here I start with implementation, because it’s difficult to write a meaningful test without a minimal outline of the logic.
sudoku.subsquares2: Tests later stabilize the function and help refine the details.
sudoku.subsquaresTests1: I create tests that verify whether the extractor returns correct 9‑element arrays.
sudoku.subsquaresTests2: This ensures that the slicing logic works as intended.
sudoku.h_debugging: Debugging with the help of tests
sudoku.debugging1: A test that should pass unexpectedly fails.
sudoku.debugging2: I begin debugging — and here tests prove invaluable:
sudoku.list1_debugging: I discover the function returns void (I forgot the return statement),
sudoku.list2_debugging: after fixing that, the test still fails — the function returns a 3‑element array instead of a 9‑element one,
sudoku.list3_debugging: tests and console.logs lead me directly to incorrect slicing ranges.
sudoku.debugging3: This is a great example of how TDD + types let me focus on architecture and logic instead of remembering every detail.
sudoku.list2_summary: architectural thinking and refactoring during development,
sudoku.list4_summary: working with algorithms and structured data,
sudoku.summary2: These are not polished portfolio snippets — this is the actual process, visible in full context.
sudoku.summary3: At the beginning I was stressed by the recording and made a few silly mistakes (quickly corrected), but later the work went smoothly.
sudoku.summary4: The timelapse shows what a real, long development process looks like — not just curated highlights.
